_README: SpaceCraft Resource Atlas — minable deposits and the items they yield, verified in-game. tier = depth/difficulty tier; bureau = lowest Mining Bureau level that locates it; yields[].primary marks the main ore vs secondary finds. NOTE: SpaceCraft's galaxy is procedurally generated, so there are no fixed system coordinates — this atlas is the canonical 'what can I mine and where does it come from' reference.

deposits.name: Cupric Stone
deposits.desc: Although it's not completely pure, this ore is only exploited for its copper, and contains no other ore that could be efficiently harvested.

deposits.name: Ferric Stone
deposits.desc: Although it's not completely pure, this ore is only exploited for its iron, and contains no other ore that could be efficiently harvested.

deposits.name: Sulphuric Stone
deposits.desc: Named after its principal component, this ore is also rich in other crystals, including pyrite and vanadium.

deposits.name: Brassy Outcrop
deposits.desc: Commonly found within the same clusters as cupric stones, these impure outcrops combine smaller copper nuggets with other crystals like quartz or azurite.

deposits.name: Coal Clump
deposits.desc: This ore contains several forms of carbonite crystals, ranging from coal and graphite to small diamonds.

deposits.name: Ferrous Outcrop
deposits.desc: Commonly found within the same clusters as ferric stones, these impure outcrops combine smaller iron nuggets with other crystals like quartz or hematite.

deposits.name: Gray Quartz
deposits.desc: The coloration of quartz is determined by its impurities. Rare impurities can actually make it more valuable, but this one just has ordinary impurities.

deposits.name: Sandstone Mound
deposits.desc: Although this sedimentary rock can be used in its current state, it also contains large quantities of quartz, aquamarine and iron, among other minerals.

deposits.name: Siderite
deposits.desc: Named after iron, its principal component, this ore is also rich in carbon and can contain quartz, graphite and hematite.

deposits.name: Aluminum Reduction
deposits.desc: Under the influence of deep submarine current, aluminium can be found in native form.

deposits.name: Bauxite Rock
deposits.desc: This sedimentary stone is incredibly rich in diverse crystals, ranging from calcite to emerald.

deposits.name: Chalcocite
deposits.desc: An especially profitable copper ore, chalcocite is rich in copper, but can also contain a large variety of other crystals.

deposits.name: Chalcopyrite
deposits.desc: Formerly known as "yellow copper", this ore is much more diverse than the name suggests, containing substantial quantities of pyrite, graphite, quartz and iron.

deposits.name: Elmerite
deposits.desc: Essential to contemporary industry, elmerium can only be found in these strange clusters that appear on the dark side of celestial objects.

deposits.name: Graphite
deposits.desc: This pure graphite ore is the most stable form of carbon.

deposits.name: Hollow Outcrop
deposits.desc: Commonly found within the same clusters as elmerite, these impure outcrops combine smaller elmerium fragments with chunks of sandstone.

deposits.name: Patronite
deposits.desc: This ore, mostly made up of vanadium and sulfur, can contain crystals ranging from quartz to diamond.

deposits.name: Pyrite Formation
deposits.desc: Pyrite is a simple and common iron sulfide. Because of its yellow shine, it has often been mistaken for gold.

deposits.name: Red Quartz
deposits.desc: The coloration of quartz is determined by its impurities. This one has exactly the kind of impurities that can make quartz more valuable.

deposits.name: Turquoise Stone
deposits.desc: Turquoise is too valuable in itself to be broken down into separate elements, even though it contains large quantities of copper and aluminum.

deposits.name: Vitreous Carbon
deposits.desc: This carbon ore contains a surprisingly high amount of graphite.

deposits.name: White Feldspar Mound
deposits.desc: This ore, mostly made up of aluminum and sandstone, can contain crystals ranging from aquamarine to emerald.

deposits.name: White Quartz
deposits.desc: The coloration of quartz is determined by its impurities. Rare impurities can actually make it more valuable, but this one has none.

deposits.name: Aquamarine
deposits.desc: Although it's not completely pure, this ore only contains crystals that are at least as valuable as pure aquamarine.

deposits.name: Augurite
deposits.desc: This volcanic rock is often rich in aluminium and titanium.

deposits.name: Azurite
deposits.desc: Azurite crystal is too valuable in itself to be broken down into separate elements, even though it contains large quantities of copper and carbon.

deposits.name: Cinnabar
deposits.desc: This mineral is a crystal of mercury sulfide. It was widely used as pigment because of its bright red color, before the high toxicity or mercury was recognized.

deposits.name: Emerald
deposits.desc: This beryl variety is too valuable in itself to be broken down into separate elements, even though it contains large quantities of aluminum and silicon.

deposits.name: Magnetite
deposits.desc: Named after its principal component, this ore is also rich in other crystals including quartz, graphite and even diamond.

deposits.name: Malachite
deposits.desc: Malachite crystal is too valuable in itself to be broken down into separate elements, even though it contains large quantities of copper and carbon.

deposits.name: Tenorite
deposits.desc: A rare and hard ore of copper, tenorite is sometimes called black copper. It often contains precious inclusions.

deposits.name: Titanite
deposits.desc: Typically found on asteroids, this ore is an aggregate of small titanium nuggets.

deposits.name: Titanomagnetite
deposits.desc: Essentially made up of iron and titanium, this ore is also rich in hematite, quartz and graphite.

deposits.name: Turquoise Clump
deposits.desc: Commonly found within the same clusters as turquoise stones, these impure clumps can contain varying proportions of aquamarine and emerald.

deposits.name: Vanadinite
deposits.desc: This common vanadium chlorure is easy to recognize with its characteristic shape and color.

deposits.name: Zircon
deposits.desc: Named after its principal component, zircon was formerly traded as a gemstone, but has become essential for industrial uses, and nuclear reactors in particular.

deposits.name: Cooperite
deposits.desc: One of the most common planetary platinium ore, cooperite is also a source of a much more mundane element, sulfur.

deposits.name: Plagnetite
deposits.desc: Generally found in asteroids, this formation contains an equal amount of iron and platinium.

deposits.name: Tungsten Reduction
deposits.desc: Under the influence of deep submarine current, tungsten can be found in native form.

deposits.name: Wolframite
deposits.desc: Wolframite get its name from wolfram, an ancient name of tungsten, its more remarkable component.

deposits.name: Uraninite Outcrop
deposits.desc: This crystalline ore is the purest natural occurrence of uranium.

deposits.name: Gigantic Diamonds
deposits.desc: Had these crystals been discovered in the Milky Way era, they would have severely disrupted the global economy.

deposits.name: Spoilers
